metadata.summary: Research-driven cycle for improving conversion rates: measure drop-offs, form hypotheses, run A/B tests to statistical significance, implement winners, document learnings.

content.title: Conversion Rate Optimization (CRO)
content.summary: A research-driven cycle for systematically improving conversion rates across web funnels: measure where users drop off, research why, form testable hypotheses, run A/B tests to statistical significance (95%+, 100+ conversions/variant, 2+ weeks), implement winners, and document learnings in a reusable library. A 1% → 2% lift doubles effective marketing spend.

why.p: Traffic without conversion is wasted acquisition budget. CRO applies scientific method to UX decisions: hypothesis + controlled test + significance gate + learning library. Without the gate, early-stop false positives corrupt the program. Without the library, the same mistakes repeat. The PIE framework (Potential × Importance × Ease) ensures effort goes to high-traffic, high-impact pages first.

list.item: Established traffic (≥5k unique visitors/page/month) with under-converting funnels.
Starting or running a structured A/B testing program.
Redesigning forms, pricing pages, checkout, or onboarding where research-driven hypotheses outperform opinion.
Quarterly CRO program reviews: wins, losses, learnings, next-cycle backlog.
Multi-page funnel diagnosis using heatmaps + session recordings + exit surveys together.
Low-traffic pages (under ~5k uniques/month) — statistical significance is unreachable; use qualitative research instead.
Pre-PMF startups — optimizing the wrong funnel; nail message-market fit first.
Stable, mature funnels with sub-1% lift potential — resources better spent on acquisition or new product.
Major redesigns — run user research and prototype testing, not A/B tests.
Compliance-heavy flows (KYC, medical) where CRO levers conflict with required disclosures.

rule.statement: Follow the research-before-testing rule: run heatmaps, session recordings, and 5+ user interviews before writing a single A/B test hypothesis.
rule.rationale: Random changes produce random results. Research anchors hypotheses to real user friction, which lifts win rate from ~30% to ~50% per test.

rule.statement: Use the PIE framework to prioritize: score each opportunity on Potential (impact headroom), Importance (traffic volume), and Ease (implementation cost), then divide by 3. Test highest-scoring opportunities first.
rule.rationale: High-traffic pages with large drop-offs and low-effort fixes return value fastest; PIE prevents teams from polishing low-traffic pages.

section.name: funnel-stage-benchmarks

rule.statement: Establish baseline metrics per funnel stage before starting any CRO work: landing page bounce rate target below 50%, signup form completion above 30%, pricing CTA click rate above 5%, checkout completion above 60%, visitor-to-customer 1-5%.
rule.rationale: Without baselines, test results have no reference frame and post-test "lift" claims are unverifiable.

section.p: Track drop-offs with funnel visualization tools (GA4, Mixpanel). Identify the single biggest leak first; fixing downstream leaks while the top leaks are open wastes budget.

section.name: hypothesis-formation

rule.statement: Every test hypothesis must follow this template: "We believe that [change] will cause [effect] because [rationale]. We will measure this by [metric] and consider it successful if [target]."
rule.rationale: Pre-registration prevents post-hoc rationalization. Hypotheses without explicit success criteria inflate false-positive rates.

section.p: Common CRO levers: clarity (simpler headlines), value prop (better benefit framing), trust (logos, testimonials), urgency (limited offers), friction reduction (fewer form fields), risk reversal (money-back guarantees).

rule.statement: Do not declare a winner until: 100+ conversions per variant, 2+ weeks of runtime covering weekly cycles, and 95%+ statistical significance. Never peek at results before the minimum sample is reached.
rule.rationale: Stopping early at ~80% confidence inflates the false-positive rate from 5% to ~30%, polluting the learning library with phantom wins.

rule.statement: Run a 30-day holdback cell (95% rollout + 5% control) after shipping winners to monitor novelty regression.
rule.rationale: Winners often regress 30+ days post-launch; holdback cells catch regressions before they fully erode the gain.

rule.statement: Keep traffic assignment user-id-hashed, not session-based, and monitor for test pollution: bot traffic, consent-banner disruption, and cross-test contamination before calling results.
rule.rationale: Session-based assignment allows the same user to see both variants; bot traffic and consent banners silently inflate or deflate exposure counts.

rule.statement: Document every test result — winner, loser, or inconclusive — with: name, date, hypothesis, change, metric result, lift percentage, what generalizes, what does not, and next test idea.
rule.rationale: Teams that maintain a learning library double their annual win rate by avoiding repeated experiments and building on prior knowledge.

example.title: Signup form: 15% → 27% completion (+45% lift)
example.p: Research: heatmaps showed hesitation at password field; session recordings showed confusion about requirements; exit surveys cited "too many fields". Hypothesis: reducing to email-only signup (set password later) will increase form completion because it removes the most-cited friction point. Result: 45% lift, no negative impact on activation.

example.title: Pricing page: 2% → 3.3% trial conversion over two sequential tests
example.p: Research: users wanted easy plan comparison; FAQ questions revealed feature confusion; mobile layout was broken. Three tests: (1) comparison table — no impact; (2) FAQ section added — +15%; (3) mobile redesign — +20% on mobile segment. All three run to full significance; mobile segmented separately from desktop.

antipattern.description: Stopping tests early when results look good at 80% confidence.
antipattern.reason: At 80% confidence the false-positive rate is ~30%, not 5%. "We have a winner" declared on day 4 pollutes the learning library with phantom gains.

antipattern.description: Using heatmaps as the only research source before forming hypotheses.
antipattern.reason: Heatmaps reveal where users click but not why they leave. Pair with session recordings and exit surveys to triangulate intent; heatmap-only hypotheses have a lower win rate.

antipattern.description: Copying "best practice" changes from industry case studies without local research.
antipattern.reason: Urgency timers and social-proof-above-fold tactics fail to replicate without local data confirming the same friction exists in your specific audience.

antipattern.description: Running a major redesign as an A/B test.
antipattern.reason: Multivariate changes cannot isolate which element caused any measured effect; use user research and prototype testing instead.

antipattern.description: No learning library entry after a test concludes — win or loss.
antipattern.reason: Without documentation, teams re-run the same failed tests and lose accumulated institutional knowledge. The library is the primary long-term asset of a CRO program.

template.purpose: CRO audit template: funnel analysis table, research findings, PIE backlog, recommended tests.
A/B test brief: hypothesis, variants, metrics, sample size, implementation checklist.
Binary metric sample-size calculator: two-sided, 95% confidence, 80% power.
